normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.requirement_title: The app secures all network traffic according to the current best practices.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-NETWORK-1 (Description): Ensuring data privacy and integrity of any data in transit is critical for any app that communicates over the network. This is typically done by encrypting data and authenticating the remote endpoint, as TLS does. However, there are many ways for a developer to disable the platform secure defaults, or bypass them completely by using low-level APIs or third-party libraries. This control ensures that the app is in fact setting up secure connections in any situation.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must protect data in transit by using platform-secure TLS defaults for every network communication. Developers must not disable platform secure defaults (NSAllowsArbitraryLoads on iOS, custom TrustManagers accepting any certificate on Android), bypass platform TLS via low-level APIs, or import third-party libraries that override platform security. Modern TLS configuration (TLS 1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 preferred) is required for app-controlled flows.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies universally at Tier 1+. Per Constitution Pillar 5 (Secure Architecture & Runtime), secure-transport discipline is universal for every Intrinsic Code app because network communication is assumed baseline (cloud sync, authentication, telemetry, content delivery). The blueprint schema does not declare a discrete network-presence field; the universal applicability is enforced via the app_tier floor instead. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 1+ performs network communication because cloud sync, authentication, telemetry, or content delivery are baseline.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 5 covers MASVS-NETWORK-1 via two layered traces: SEC-PILLAR-5-SECURE-TRANSPORT (primary, Tier 1+ baseline) and SEC-PILLAR-5-CERTIFICATE-PINNING (Tier 4+ supporting). SECURE-TRANSPORT enforces the baseline (TLS defaults, no plaintext HTTP for app-controlled flows, no unsafe validation overrides, modern TLS configuration declared in manifest); CERTIFICATE-PINNING extends to identity pinning at Tier 4+ for high-assurance backend communication. MASVS-NETWORK-1's scope is the universal best-practices baseline; SEC-PILLAR-5-SECURE-TRANSPORT directly implements this at Tier 1+. Implementation correctness for TLS configuration (cipher suite selection, certificate chain validation, server hostname verification) and the absence of accidental overrides (NSAllowsArbitraryLoads, custom TrustManagers) requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. The mapping is partial because no single Constitution control captures the full breadth of MASVS-NETWORK-1 at every tier, but the two-trace coverage substantively addresses the baseline and the Tier 4+ extension.

expected_evidence_patterns: iOS: App Transport Security (ATS) configuration declared in Info.plist with no NSAllowsArbitraryLoads = true; any per-domain exception explicitly justified in security_manifest
Android: Network Security Configuration declared in AndroidManifest.xml + res/xml/network_security_config.xml; cleartextTrafficPermitted = false for production builds
Network calls use platform HTTPS clients (URLSession on iOS, OkHttp / HttpsURLConnection on Android) with default TLS configuration — not custom SSLContext or TrustManager bypasses
Modern TLS configuration: minimum TLS 1.2, TLS 1.3 preferred where supported; cipher suites restricted to FORWARD-SECRECY-capable suites
Tier 4+: certificate pinning declared in manifest with pin set, pin rotation policy, and backup pin discipline (per SEC-PILLAR-5-CERTIFICATE-PINNING)
Third-party SDKs verified to use platform secure transport — no SDK that imports its own TLS stack or accepts cleartext
failure_indicators: iOS: NSAllowsArbitraryLoads = true in Info.plist without per-domain exception justification (effectively disables ATS for the entire app)
Android: cleartextTrafficPermitted = true in network_security_config.xml without explicit per-domain scope (allows plaintext HTTP for any host)
Custom TrustManager that accepts all certificates: TrustManager.checkServerTrusted() returning silently without validation (allows MITM via any certificate)
Custom HostnameVerifier that returns true unconditionally (defeats hostname verification, allows MITM via any valid certificate)
Use of low-level socket APIs (NWConnection without TLS, Socket without SSLSocket wrapping) for sensitive data transport
Plaintext HTTP transport for any app-controlled flow carrying user data, authentication tokens, or app state
TLS 1.0 or TLS 1.1 explicitly allowed in cipher suite configuration (deprecated by RFC 8996)
false_positive_traps: Network calls inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test mocks legitimately use HTTP mock servers; these are not production violations.

OS-level platform-owned flows (captive portal detection, system update checks, OCSP/CRL fetches by the TLS stack itself) are out of scope for this control per the SECURE-TRANSPORT trace boundary — the violation surface is app-controlled flows only.

Plaintext HTTP to localhost (127.0.0.1, ::1) for IPC between app processes is NOT a NETWORK-1 violation — loopback traffic doesn't leave the device. Localhost flows belong to MASVS-PLATFORM-1 (IPC) instead.

Per-domain ATS exceptions on iOS or per-domain Network Security Configuration exceptions on Android are NOT violations if explicitly justified in security_manifest with documented reason (e.g., legacy partner API with HTTPS rollout pending). The applicability condition catches universal disablement, not scoped exceptions.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 5 secure-transport discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-SECURE-TRANSPORT) as primary evidence toward MASVS-NETWORK-1 baseline coverage at Tier 1+
uses Constitution Pillar 5 certificate-pinning discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-CERTIFICATE-PINNING) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-NETWORK-1 high-assurance coverage at Tier 4+
enforces platform-secure TLS defaults (App Transport Security on iOS, Network Security Configuration on Android) per MASVS-NETWORK-1 baseline requirement
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-NETWORK-1. MASVS-NETWORK-1 is mapped to two Constitution v0.5 traces in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-5-SECURE-TRANSPORT (primary, Tier 1+ baseline) and SEC-PILLAR-5-CERTIFICATE-PINNING (supporting, Tier 4+ identity-pinning addition). OS-level platform-owned flows (captive portal, system updates, TLS stack's own OCSP/CRL fetches) are out of scope for this control. Claims about resistance to nation-state-level TLS attacks (CA compromise, BGP-level traffic hijacking) are out of scope unless certificate pinning is in effect at Tier 4+. Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
